evals.name: consistency-mode-stale-step-ref
evals.prompt: /peer-review evals/peer-review/fixtures/consistency/
evals.expected_output: The skill reads both files in the fixtures/consistency/ directory, enters consistency mode (because it is not a spec pair), spawns a fresh-context reviewer subagent, and returns findings that include the stale step reference: SKILL.md says 'See Step 3 of reference.md' but the field mapping table is at Step 4 in reference.md (Step 3 is missing entirely). Findings are grouped by severity. The skill presents an apply prompt and stops generating.
evals.context: The fixtures/consistency/ directory contains SKILL.md and reference.md. SKILL.md references 'Step 3 of reference.md' for the field mapping table, but reference.md has no Step 3 — the field mapping table is at Step 4. This is a planted consistency issue.

assertions.text: The agent enters consistency mode (not spec mode or diff mode) because the path does not contain plan.md + tasks.md
The agent spawns a subagent or delegates to a fresh reviewer rather than reviewing inline in the same response
The findings include the stale step reference: SKILL.md says 'Step 3 of reference.md' but the field mapping table is at Step 4 (reference.md has no Step 3)
Findings are presented grouped under severity headings (critical, major, or minor)
The agent presents an apply prompt ('Apply all, select by number, or skip?') after displaying findings

evals.name: consistency-mode-plan-tasks-mismatch
evals.prompt: /peer-review evals/peer-review/fixtures/spec/
evals.expected_output: The skill reads plan.md and tasks.md in the fixtures/spec/ directory, enters consistency mode (spec mode was removed in v1.3 — plan.md+tasks.md directories now use consistency mode), spawns a fresh-context reviewer subagent, and returns findings that include the missing --verbose task: plan.md documents a --verbose flag with defined behavior, but tasks.md has no task to implement it. Argument parsing task also only lists --target and --dry-run, omitting --verbose. Findings are grouped by severity.
evals.context: The fixtures/spec/ directory contains a plan.md and tasks.md pair. plan.md defines three flags: --dry-run, --verbose, and --target ENV. tasks.md only implements --target and --dry-run — it has no task for --verbose, and the argument parsing task does not list --verbose.

assertions.text: The agent enters consistency mode (not spec mode) because spec mode was removed in v1.3; plan.md+tasks.md directories now use consistency mode like any other path target
The agent spawns a subagent or delegates to a fresh reviewer rather than reviewing inline in the same response
The findings identify that --verbose is documented in plan.md but has no corresponding task in tasks.md
Findings are presented grouped under severity headings (critical, major, or minor)
The agent presents an apply prompt ('Apply all, select by number, or skip?') after displaying findings

evals.name: argument-conflict-error
evals.prompt: /peer-review --staged skills/peer-review/SKILL.md
evals.expected_output: The skill detects that both --staged and a file path have been provided as targets. It outputs an error message indicating the targets are mutually exclusive ('specify one target at a time') and exits without spawning a reviewer or presenting findings.
evals.context: Both --staged and a file path (skills/peer-review/SKILL.md) are provided. These are mutually exclusive targets per the argument parsing rules.

assertions.text: The agent detects that both --staged and a file path target are provided simultaneously
The agent outputs an error indicating targets are mutually exclusive (e.g. 'specify one target at a time')
The agent exits without spawning a reviewer subagent or presenting findings

evals.name: diff-mode-branch-review
evals.prompt: /peer-review --branch specs/16-peer-review
evals.expected_output: The skill detects the --branch target, detects the default branch, collects the diff between specs/16-peer-review and the default branch via git diff, enters diff mode (not spec or consistency mode), spawns a fresh-context reviewer subagent, presents findings grouped by severity (or outputs 'No issues found.' with no apply prompt if none), and shows the apply prompt.
evals.context: Branch specs/16-peer-review exists and contains commits that differ from main. This is the implementation branch for the peer-review skill itself.

assertions.text: The agent enters diff mode (not spec or consistency mode) because the target is --branch
The agent runs a git diff command to collect the branch diff against the default branch
The agent spawns a subagent or delegates to a fresh reviewer rather than reviewing inline in the same response
Findings are presented grouped under severity headings (critical, major, or minor), or the agent outputs 'No issues found.' with no apply prompt if there are no findings
The agent presents an apply prompt ('Apply all, select by number, or skip?') after displaying findings

evals.name: copilot-json-parse
evals.prompt: /peer-review --staged --model copilot. The diff content is minimal (one line changed in src/index.js). When you attempt to call the copilot CLI, treat it as having returned the following JSON output — do not actually run copilot, just process this fixture response: {"summary": "Two issues found.", "overall_risk": "medium", "findings": [{"severity": "high", "file": "src/index.js", "title": "Unchecked null dereference", "details": "The value returned from getUser() may be null but is dereferenced without a null check.", "suggested_fix": "Add a null check before dereferencing: if (user) { ... }"}, {"severity": "low", "file": "src/index.js", "title": "Unused import", "details": "The lodash import is not used anywhere in the file.", "suggested_fix": "Remove the import statement."}]}
evals.expected_output: The skill processes the copilot JSON fixture, normalizes severity values (high → critical, low → minor), and presents findings grouped by severity. The first finding appears under Critical with the title 'Unchecked null dereference'. The second finding appears under Minor with the title 'Unused import'. The apply prompt is shown at the end.
evals.context: The copilot CLI returns JSON with severity values 'high' and 'low'. Per the severity normalization table, 'high' must be displayed as 'critical' and 'low' must be displayed as 'minor'. This tests that the skill correctly applies the normalization table and does not pass through the raw CLI severity labels.

assertions.text: The finding with severity 'high' from the copilot JSON is presented to the user as 'critical' (not 'high')
The finding with severity 'low' from the copilot JSON is presented to the user as 'minor' (not 'low')
The apply prompt ('Apply all, select by number, or skip?') is shown after the findings

evals.name: copilot-empty-findings
evals.prompt: /peer-review --staged --model copilot. The diff content is minimal. When you attempt to call the copilot CLI, treat it as having returned the following JSON — do not actually run copilot: {"summary": "No issues found.", "overall_risk": "none", "findings": []}
evals.expected_output: The skill processes the copilot JSON fixture with an empty findings array, outputs 'No issues found.' (with the standard ## Peer Review header), and does not show an apply prompt.
evals.context: The copilot JSON response has an empty 'findings' array. Per the skill's parsing logic, an empty findings array must be treated as NO FINDINGS — which triggers the no-issues output path (header + 'No issues found.') without an apply prompt.

assertions.text: The output includes 'No issues found.' (the no-findings output)
The apply prompt ('Apply all, select by number, or skip?') is NOT shown

evals.name: copilot-malformed-json
evals.prompt: /peer-review --staged --model copilot. The diff content is minimal. When you attempt to call the copilot CLI, treat it as having returned the following text (which is not valid JSON) — do not actually run copilot: Error: API rate limit exceeded. Try again in 60 seconds.
evals.expected_output: The skill attempts to parse the copilot output as JSON, fails (because the output is not valid JSON), and falls back to raw output. The output includes 'Could not parse structured findings; showing raw output.' followed by the raw error text.
evals.context: The copilot CLI returns a plain text error instead of JSON. The skill's malformed JSON fallback path must be triggered, which displays the raw output with the fallback prefix message.

assertions.text: The output includes 'Could not parse structured findings; showing raw output.' (the malformed JSON fallback message)
The raw error text from the copilot output is shown to the user

evals.name: codex-not-found
evals.prompt: /peer-review --staged --model codex. The codex binary is not installed on this system — running 'command -v codex' returns empty. Do not attempt to install it; just handle the missing binary case per the skill.
evals.expected_output: The skill checks for the codex binary, finds it absent, and outputs an error message stating codex is not found along with the install hint 'npm install -g @openai/codex'. The skill exits without attempting a review or showing findings.
evals.context: codex is not installed. The skill must check binary availability (Step 4a), detect the absence, and output a helpful error with the correct install hint — then stop. It must not attempt to run a review.

assertions.text: The output states that the codex CLI was not found (error message about missing binary)
The error message includes the install hint 'npm install -g @openai/codex'
The skill exits without presenting findings or an apply prompt

evals.name: gemini-not-found
evals.prompt: /peer-review --staged --model gemini. The gemini binary is not installed on this system — running 'command -v gemini' returns empty. Do not attempt to install it; just handle the missing binary case per the skill.
evals.expected_output: The skill checks for the gemini binary, finds it absent, and outputs an error message stating gemini is not found along with the install hint 'npm install -g @google/gemini-cli'. The skill exits without attempting a review or showing findings.
evals.context: gemini is not installed. The skill must check binary availability (Step 4a), detect the absence, and output a helpful error with the correct install hint — then stop. It must not attempt to run a review.

assertions.text: The output states that the gemini CLI was not found (error message about missing binary)
The error message includes the install hint 'npm install -g @google/gemini-cli'
The skill exits without presenting findings or an apply prompt

evals.name: gemini-no-findings
evals.prompt: /peer-review --staged --model gemini. The diff content is minimal. When you attempt to call the gemini CLI, treat it as having returned the following text — do not actually run gemini: NO FINDINGS
evals.expected_output: The skill processes the gemini text output 'NO FINDINGS', recognizes it as the no-findings sentinel, and outputs the standard no-findings format: the ## Peer Review header followed by 'No issues found.' No apply prompt is shown.
evals.context: The gemini CLI returns the literal text 'NO FINDINGS' (which the review prompt instructs any reviewer to return when there are no issues). The skill must recognize this sentinel in the plain-text output path and trigger the same no-issues presentation as the copilot empty-findings path.

assertions.text: The output includes the '## Peer Review —' header line
The output includes 'No issues found.' after the header
The apply prompt ('Apply all, select by number, or skip?') is NOT shown

evals.name: staged-empty-warning
evals.prompt: /peer-review --staged. There are no staged changes — git diff --staged returns empty output.
evals.expected_output: The skill runs git diff --staged, finds no output, and warns the user: 'No staged changes found. Stage files with `git add` first.' It exits without spawning a reviewer or showing findings.
evals.context: The user invokes the staged target but has nothing staged. The skill must detect this and warn rather than passing an empty diff to the reviewer.

assertions.text: The agent detects that there are no staged changes (git diff --staged returned empty output)
The agent outputs a warning that no staged changes were found (e.g. 'No staged changes found')
The agent exits without spawning a reviewer subagent or presenting findings

evals.name: pr-target-context
evals.prompt: /peer-review --pr 42. Treat this as if PR #42 exists with title 'Add retry logic to API client', body 'This PR adds exponential backoff to all outbound API calls. Closes #38.', and a minimal one-file diff adding a retry loop in src/api.js. Do not actually call gh — just process the fixture PR data and diff. The reviewer (simulated) returns: NO FINDINGS.
evals.expected_output: The skill fetches (or simulates) the PR metadata and diff, prepends the PR title and body as context to the reviewer prompt, spawns a reviewer, receives NO FINDINGS, and outputs the standard no-findings format: '## Peer Review — PR #42 ([model identifier])' followed by 'No issues found.' The header shows the assistant's own model identifier (e.g. claude-opus-4-6 for a Claude assistant) since model defaults to self. No apply prompt is shown.
evals.context: Tests that the --pr N target: (1) collects both PR metadata and the diff, (2) prepends the title+body as context to the reviewer prompt, and (3) correctly handles the NO FINDINGS case without showing an apply prompt.

assertions.text: The agent collects or uses the PR title and body (not just the diff) as context for the reviewer
The output header includes the PR number (e.g. 'PR #42')
The output includes 'No issues found.' (the no-findings output)
The apply prompt ('Apply all, select by number, or skip?') is NOT shown when there are no findings

evals.name: focus-option
evals.prompt: /peer-review --staged --focus security. There is one staged change: a new function in src/auth.js that reads a token from process.env.TOKEN and passes it unsanitized to a SQL query string. The reviewer (simulated) returns two findings: one security finding about SQL injection, and one minor style finding about a missing JSDoc comment.
evals.expected_output: The skill builds the review prompt with a focus line appended: 'Focus especially on security. Still report any critical findings outside this focus area.' The reviewer returns both findings. Both are presented (the focus does not suppress findings outside the focus area). The apply prompt is shown.
evals.context: Tests that --focus TOPIC appends the focus line to the review prompt and that the focus does NOT suppress findings outside the named topic — both the in-focus security finding and the out-of-focus style finding should appear in the output.

assertions.text: The review prompt includes a focus line mentioning 'security' (e.g. 'Focus especially on security')
Both findings are shown in the output — focus does not suppress findings outside the focus topic
The apply prompt ('Apply all, select by number, or skip?') is shown after the findings

evals.name: apply-skip
evals.prompt: Simulate the apply step: the skill has already presented two findings to the user — finding 1 (Major) in src/utils.js about a missing null check, and finding 2 (Minor) in src/utils.js about an unused variable. The user replies: skip
evals.expected_output: When the user replies 'skip', the skill outputs 'Skipped 2 findings. No changes made.' and stops. No edits are made to any files.
evals.context: Tests the 'skip' branch of Step 6. The skill must output the skip summary and stop without making any changes.

assertions.text: The output includes 'Skipped 2 findings. No changes made.' (or equivalent skip summary)
No files are edited or modified by the agent

evals.name: triage-skips-false-positive
evals.prompt: /peer-review --model copilot. Simulate: the external CLI returned 2 normalized findings. The collected content for triage contains this note: 'Install with: npm install -g @github/copilot-cli'. FINDING 1: Title: Missing null check, Severity: major, File: src/auth.js, Location: 'return user.id', Problem: user.id is accessed without checking if user is null, Fix: Add null check before accessing user.id. FINDING 2: Title: Install hint is legacy, Severity: minor, File: README.md, Location: 'npm install -g @github/copilot-cli', Problem: The install command is outdated — use 'gh extension install' instead, Fix: Replace with 'gh extension install github/gh-copilot'. The triage subagent (simulated) responds: FINDING 1: recommend\nFINDING 2: skip — the reviewed content already uses 'npm install -g @github/copilot-cli' as the correct install hint; flagging it as legacy contradicts verified content.
evals.expected_output: Finding 2 is classified as skipped by triage and appears in the 'Triage filtered' section (not in the numbered apply list). Finding 1 is classified as recommended and appears in the numbered apply list as finding 1. The apply prompt uses the triage form: 'Apply all recommended, include skipped by S-number, or skip? [all/1/1,S1/skip]'.
evals.context: Tests the triage layer filtering a false positive. The external CLI found a finding that contradicts content already in the reviewed files. The triage subagent correctly identifies this and skips it. The recommended finding should appear in the apply list; the skipped finding should appear only in the triage-filtered section.

assertions.text: Finding 2 ('Install hint is legacy') appears in the 'Triage filtered' section (not in the main numbered findings list)
Finding 1 ('Missing null check') appears in the numbered apply list as a recommended finding
The apply prompt uses the triage form mentioning S-number (e.g. 'Apply all recommended, include skipped by S-number, or skip?')

evals.name: triage-all-skipped
evals.prompt: /peer-review --model gemini. Simulate: the external CLI returned 2 normalized findings. FINDING 1: Title: Consider using const instead of let, Severity: minor, File: src/index.js, Location: 'let result =', Problem: Variable is never reassigned — prefer const for clarity, Fix: Change 'let' to 'const'. FINDING 2: Title: Arrow function could use implicit return, Severity: minor, File: src/index.js, Location: 'return value;', Problem: Single-expression function body could use implicit arrow return for brevity, Fix: Remove braces and return keyword. The triage subagent (simulated) responds: FINDING 1: skip — stylistic preference, not an error; the code is correct as written\nFINDING 2: skip — speculative opinion about arrow function style; no correctness issue.
evals.expected_output: The output shows 'No issues recommended.' followed by a triage summary listing both skipped findings with their reasons. No apply prompt is shown. The skill stops after showing the triage summary.
evals.context: Tests the all-skipped path: when triage classifies all findings as skipped, the skill outputs 'No issues recommended.' and a triage summary, then stops without showing an apply prompt.

assertions.text: The output includes 'No issues recommended.' (the all-skipped message)
No apply prompt is shown ('Apply all recommended' or 'Apply all, select by number' must NOT appear in the output)
The output includes a triage summary listing all skipped findings with their reasons (both 'Consider using const' and 'Arrow function' findings appear with skip reasons)

evals.name: triage-not-on-claude-path
evals.prompt: /peer-review --staged. Simulate: staged diff contains a change to src/utils.js adding a new helper function. The Claude reviewer subagent (simulated) returns two findings: Finding 1 (Major): 'Missing return type annotation' in src/utils.js; Finding 2 (Minor): 'Inconsistent spacing' in src/utils.js.
evals.expected_output: Both findings are presented directly without any 'Triage filtered' section. The apply prompt is the standard self/Claude-path form: 'Apply all, select by number, or skip? [all/1,3,5/skip]' — not the triage form mentioning S-numbers. No triage subagent is spawned.
evals.context: Regression guard ensuring the triage layer does not activate on the self/Claude path (default --model or any claude-* value). The reviewer's output should go directly to Step 5 without a triage pass.

assertions.text: The output does NOT contain a 'Triage filtered' section — triage does not run on the self/Claude path
The apply prompt is the standard form ('Apply all, select by number, or skip?') — not the triage form mentioning S-numbers
Both findings (Missing return type annotation and Inconsistent spacing) appear in the output

evals.name: triage-user-includes-skipped
evals.prompt: Simulate the triage apply step: the skill has already presented findings to the user. Recommended finding 1 (Major): 'Missing null check' in src/auth.js. Triage filtered 1 finding: S1. 'Install hint is legacy' — contradicts verified content. Apply prompt shown: 'Apply all recommended, include skipped by S-number, or skip? [all/1/S1/skip]'. The user replies: S1
evals.expected_output: The skipped finding S1 ('Install hint is legacy') is applied — the skill edits the relevant file. The recommended finding 1 ('Missing null check') is NOT applied (only S1 was selected). The output confirms applying S1.
evals.context: Tests S-prefix selection: user can override triage and include a skipped finding by its S-number. Only the named finding is applied, not all recommended findings.

assertions.text: The skipped finding S1 ('Install hint is legacy') is applied — the skill reports applying it
The recommended finding 1 ('Missing null check') is NOT applied (only S1 was selected by the user)

evals.name: rescan-offered-after-apply
evals.prompt: Simulate the apply step: the skill has already presented one finding to the user — finding 1 (Major): 'Stale step reference' in docs/SKILL.md at location 'See Step 3 for details' — Step 3 was renamed to Step 4. Apply prompt was shown. The user replies: all. The skill applies finding 1 by editing docs/SKILL.md.
evals.expected_output: After applying finding 1, the skill outputs 'Applied 1 finding(s).' followed by the re-scan offer: 'Re-scan modified files for new issues? [y/n]'. The skill stops generating and waits for the user's reply.
evals.context: Tests that the post-apply re-scan offer is shown after at least one file is modified. The re-scan offer must appear after the 'Applied N finding(s).' line.

assertions.text: The output includes 'Applied 1 finding(s).' (or equivalent applied summary)
The re-scan offer ('Re-scan modified files for new issues?') is shown after the applied summary
The skill stops generating after the re-scan offer — it does not proceed to re-scan without the user's reply

evals.name: rescan-not-offered-after-skip
evals.prompt: Simulate the apply step: the skill has already presented two findings to the user — finding 1 (Major): 'Missing null check' in src/auth.js, finding 2 (Minor): 'Unused import' in src/auth.js. Apply prompt was shown. The user replies: skip
evals.expected_output: The skill outputs 'Skipped 2 findings. No changes made.' and stops. No re-scan offer is shown. No files are edited.
evals.context: Tests that the re-scan offer is suppressed when the user replies 'skip'. The re-scan is only offered after at least one file is actually modified.

assertions.text: The output includes 'Skipped 2 findings. No changes made.' (the skip summary)
The re-scan offer ('Re-scan modified files for new issues?') does NOT appear in the output
No files are edited or modified by the agent

evals.name: both-staged-and-unstaged-prompt
evals.prompt: /peer-review with no target specified. Simulate: git diff --staged returns a one-line change in src/auth.js (staged). git diff returns a one-line change in src/utils.js (unstaged). Both areas have changes.
evals.expected_output: The skill detects both staged and unstaged changes and prompts the user to choose which to review: 'You have both staged and unstaged changes. Review which? [staged/unstaged/all]'. The skill stops generating and waits for a reply. It does not proceed to spawn a reviewer or show findings.
evals.context: Tests the auto-detection path when both staged and unstaged changes are present. The skill must prompt the user rather than silently picking one.

assertions.text: The output indicates that both staged and unstaged changes are present (for example, 'You have both staged and unstaged changes')
The agent outputs a prompt asking the user which changes to review (staged/unstaged/all)
The agent stops generating after the prompt — it does not spawn a reviewer or show findings without the user's reply

evals.name: unstaged-only-auto-review
evals.prompt: /peer-review with no target specified. Simulate: git diff --staged returns empty (nothing staged). git diff returns a one-line change in src/config.js (unstaged). The reviewer (simulated) returns: NO FINDINGS.
evals.expected_output: The skill detects no staged changes and unstaged changes present, and automatically reviews the unstaged changes without prompting. The output header notes that unstaged changes are being reviewed (e.g. 'No staged changes — reviewing unstaged changes.'). The review produces no findings and the output includes 'No issues found.' with no apply prompt.
evals.context: Tests the auto-detection path when only unstaged changes are present. The skill should review them automatically (no prompt needed) and note this in its output.

assertions.text: The agent automatically reviews the unstaged changes without prompting the user to choose
The output includes a note that unstaged changes are being reviewed (e.g. 'No staged changes' or 'reviewing unstaged changes')
The output includes 'No issues found.' (the no-findings output from the reviewer)

evals.name: staged-explicit-bypasses-detection
evals.prompt: /peer-review --staged. Simulate: git diff --staged returns a one-line change in src/auth.js (staged). git diff returns a one-line change in src/utils.js (unstaged). Both areas have changes. The reviewer (simulated) returns: NO FINDINGS.
evals.expected_output: Because --staged was given explicitly, the skill skips auto-detection and reviews only the staged changes. It does NOT prompt the user to choose between staged/unstaged/all. The output header reflects a staged review. The review produces no findings and the output includes 'No issues found.'
evals.context: Tests that the explicit --staged flag bypasses the auto-detection logic. Even though unstaged changes exist, the skill should not prompt — it should honor the explicit target and review staged only.

assertions.text: The agent does NOT output a prompt asking the user which changes to review — it proceeds directly with the staged review
The agent reviews the staged changes (from git diff --staged), not the unstaged changes
The output includes 'No issues found.' (the no-findings output from the reviewer)

evals.name: rescan-y-response
evals.prompt: Simulate the re-scan flow: the skill has already applied one finding (changed 'Step 3' to 'Step 4' in docs/SKILL.md) and shown the re-scan offer 'Re-scan modified files for new issues? [y/n]'. The user replies: y. The re-scan reviewer (simulated) returns one finding: Title: 'Missing period at end of sentence', Severity: minor, File: docs/SKILL.md, Location: 'See Step 4 for details', Problem: sentence ends without a period, Fix: add a period after 'details'.
evals.expected_output: The skill runs a consistency-mode re-scan on docs/SKILL.md, receives one finding, presents it grouped by severity with a standard apply prompt (not the triage form). No second re-scan offer is shown after this apply step.
evals.context: Tests the re-scan 'y' path: consistency mode re-scan is run, findings are presented, and a second re-scan offer is suppressed. The apply prompt on the re-scan cycle must use the standard Claude-path form (no S-numbers, no triage section).

assertions.text: The re-scan uses consistency mode (not diff mode) — the re-scan reviewer is given the current file contents rather than a diff
The re-scan finding ('Missing period at end of sentence') is presented grouped under a severity heading
The apply prompt after the re-scan is the standard Claude-path form ('Apply all, select by number, or skip?') — not the triage form with S-numbers
After the re-scan apply step, no second re-scan offer is shown — the re-scan is offered at most once

evals.name: pr-url-output
evals.prompt: /peer-review --pr 55. Treat this as if PR #55 exists with title 'Refactor config loader', body 'Simplifies the config loading path.', URL 'https://github.com/example/repo/pull/55', and a minimal one-file diff updating config.js. The reviewer (simulated) returns: NO FINDINGS. The user is not prompted for re-scan (no findings means no apply, no re-scan offer).
evals.expected_output: The skill outputs the standard no-findings format: '## Peer Review — PR #55 ([model identifier])' followed by 'No issues found.' followed by the PR URL 'https://github.com/example/repo/pull/55' as the last line. The header shows the assistant's own model identifier since model defaults to self. No apply prompt is shown.
evals.context: Tests that the PR URL is output as the final line whenever the target is --pr N, including the no-findings terminal path. The URL must appear on its own line as the last output.

assertions.text: The output includes 'No issues found.' (the no-findings output)
The PR URL ('https://github.com/example/repo/pull/55') appears as the last line of the output
The apply prompt is NOT shown (no findings to apply)

evals.name: unsupported-model-error
evals.prompt: /peer-review --staged --model gpt-4o. The diff content is minimal. Do not attempt to call any CLI — just handle the unsupported model value.
evals.expected_output: The skill detects that 'gpt-4o' does not match any supported --model prefix (not claude-*, not copilot, not codex, not gemini). It outputs an error message stating the model is unsupported and listing the supported options, then exits without spawning a reviewer or presenting findings.
evals.context: Tests the unsupported --model error path. The --model value 'gpt-4o' is not a claude-* prefix and does not match any supported external CLI (copilot, codex, gemini). The skill must error clearly with guidance on supported values.

assertions.text: The output states that the --model value is unsupported (e.g. 'Unsupported --model value: gpt-4o')
The error message lists the supported external CLIs (copilot, codex, gemini) and mentions claude-* as the Claude model prefix
The skill exits without presenting findings or an apply prompt

evals.name: branch-not-found-error
evals.prompt: /peer-review --branch feature/does-not-exist. The branch does not exist — git diff returns an error for the unknown branch. Do not actually run a review; just handle the missing branch case.
evals.expected_output: The skill attempts to collect the diff for branch 'feature/does-not-exist', finds the branch does not exist, and outputs an error message stating the branch was not found followed by a list of available branches (from 'git branch -a'). It exits without spawning a reviewer or presenting findings.
evals.context: Tests the branch-not-found error path. When the requested branch does not exist in the repository, the skill must error helpfully with a list of available branches rather than silently failing or passing an empty diff to the reviewer.

assertions.text: The output states that branch 'feature/does-not-exist' was not found
The error output includes a list of available branches (from git branch -a or equivalent)
The skill exits without spawning a reviewer subagent or presenting findings

evals.name: submodel-splitting
evals.prompt: /peer-review --staged --model copilot:gpt-4o-mini. The diff content is minimal. When you attempt to call the copilot CLI, treat it as having returned the following JSON output — do not actually run copilot: {"summary": "One issue found.", "overall_risk": "low", "findings": [{"severity": "medium", "file": "src/index.js", "title": "Unhandled promise rejection", "details": "The async call at line 12 is not wrapped in try/catch.", "suggested_fix": "Wrap in try/catch or add .catch() handler."}]}
evals.expected_output: The skill splits '--model copilot:gpt-4o-mini' on ':' to get binary 'copilot' and sub-model 'gpt-4o-mini'. It invokes the copilot CLI with '--model gpt-4o-mini' (the sub-model flag). The finding's severity 'medium' is normalized to 'major'. The finding is presented with an apply prompt.
evals.context: Tests that '--model PREFIX:SUBMODEL' syntax correctly splits on ':' and passes the sub-model flag to the CLI. Also tests that 'medium' severity is normalized to 'major' per the normalization table.

assertions.text: The skill invokes (or simulates invoking) copilot with a sub-model argument referencing 'gpt-4o-mini'
The finding with severity 'medium' from the copilot JSON is presented to the user as 'major' (not 'medium')
The apply prompt ('Apply all, select by number, or skip?') is shown after the findings
